turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: The second case I have to announce is the Fourth Estate Public Benefit Corp. v. Wall-Street.com, number 17-571. §411(a) of the Copyright Act bars a copyright claimant from instituting suit for infringement until registration has been made. Duly readings of this prescription have been tendered by the parties and advanced by Courts. The application approach under which registration occurs as soon as the claimant submits to the copyright office a proper application for registration, and the registration approach under which registration has been made only after the copyright office reviews and registers the copyright. Petitioner in the case before us, Fourth Estate Public Benefit Corp. licensed journalism works to Wall-Street.com, a news website. When Wall-Street canceled the license agreement, but continued to display on its website articles produced by Fourth Estate, Fourth Estate sued Wall-Street and its owner for copyright infringement. Although Fourth Estate had filed applications to register the articles in question, when Fourth Estate commenced suit, the register of copyrights had not yet acted on those applications. Adopting the registration approach, the District Court dismissed Fourth Estate's complaint and the United States Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit affirmed the dismissal. We agree with the Courts below. Registration occurs and a copyright claimant may sue for infringement we hold today, not when a copyright claimant applies for registration, but only when the copyright office in fact acts on the application. Our decision follows from §411(a)'s text. The provision's first sentence states that no infringement action shall be instituted until preregistration or registration of the copyright claim has been made. The second sentence states an exception to the main rule declared in the first sentence. When the required deposit application and fee have been delivered to the copyright office in proper form and registration has been refused, the claimant may institute an action for infringement if notice thereof is served on the register. If a claimant's application suffice to accomplish registration, §411(a)'s second sentence allowing suit upon the copyright office's refusal of registration would be superfluous. What utility would that allowance have if a copyright claimant could sue for infringement immediately after applying for registration without awaiting the register's decision on her application? We note as well that Congress provided a preregistration option for certain works especially vulnerable to pre-distribution infringement. That option too would have diminished utility if a completed application qualified as registration. Fourth Estate insists that the Copyright Act uses the phrases make registration and registration has been made to describe action by the copyright claimant. As Fourth Estate acknowledges however, the statute, at least sometimes, uses registration to mean action by the register. We must look to the specific context of the terms used, Fourth Estate agrees, to decide what the term means in a particular provision. The specific context of §411(a) as just explained permits only one sensible reading, registration has been made only when the copyright office in fact grants registration. Fourth Estate also argues that because the copyright owner gains exclusive rights on the creation of her work, suits for infringement should be treated as independent of action by the copyright office. But a copyright owner may eventually recover damages dating from the onset of infringement. Under §411(a), she must await the copyright office's decision on her registration application before instituting a suit. But at that point she may gain complete relief, damages for past infringement, capture of the infringer's profits, and injunction against continued violation of her exclusive right, and an order requiring the infringer to destroy infringing material. True, registration processing times have increased from one to two weeks in 1956 to many months today. So infringement may continue for some time before a copyright owner can stop it. Delays in large part are the result of copyright office staffing and budgetary shortages that Congress can alleviate, but Courts cannot fix. Unfortunate as the current administrative lag may be, that factor does not allow us to revise §411(a)'s congressionally composed text. For the reasons just summarized and more fully developed in the Court's opinion, we affirm the Eleventh Circuit's judgment. The Court's opinion is unanimous.
